coredata.dc:title: Cu2+-functionalized Zr-MOF triggers O-phenylendiamine oxidation for ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of nerve agent simulant
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, You
Gao, Yuting
Xiao, Yi
Huang, Pengcheng
Wu, Fang-Ying
coredata.dc:description: Developing a fluorescence sensor capable of precisely identifying highly toxic nerve agents has become a focus of research, given that they are easily available and can be used by terrorists to threaten the security of the world. In this work, we designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform with Cu2+-modified UiO-66-NH2 (Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2) and o-phenenediamine (OPD) as signal indicators and successfully used for the liquid and gas detection of the nerve agent simulant diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP). UiO-66-NH2 obtained oxidase activity by simple Cu2+ modification, which catalyzed the oxidation of OPD to give a yellow fluorescent product 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP). In this detection system, the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2 was greatly enhanced by blocking the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) process inside the framework by DCP. At the same time, the amino group of DAP was phosphorylated by DCP, and the attenuation of DAP fluorescence was observed by the static quenching effect. In addition, the Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD loaded agarose hydrogel device can be used to detect DCP vapor with naked eyes and showed satisfactory detection result (LOD: 6.65 ppb).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD
Diethyl chlorophosphate
Gas detection
Agarose hydrogel
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence analysis of DCP in aqueous solution
DCP vapor detection using Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD hydrogels
Luminescence and absorption properties of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD
Spectral response of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD to DCP in solution
Sensing mechanism of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD for DCP Detection
Detection of DCP vapor by Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogel

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 22164013 , 21864018 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20212BAB203033 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, (b) Particle size distribution diagram of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, (c) Elemental mapping of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, (d) PXRD pattern of simulate UiO-66-NH2, UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2.
(a) XPS profiles of UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s, (c) FT-IR pattern of UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2.
(a) Emission spectra of OPD, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD+DCP and DAP (Ex: 255 nm), (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD and DAP.

(a) Emission spectra of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at different DCP concentrations. Inset: the fluorescence color of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD varied with DCP concentration (0–80 μM), (b) Linear plot of the relative fluorescence intensity of the emission peak (F450 nm/F570 nm) versus the concentration of DCP, (c) Fluorescence spectra of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD after reaction with different kinds of organophosphorus compounds, (d) Relative fluorescence intensities of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD after reacted with DCP and other organophosphorus compounds (F450 nm/F570 nm). Inset: fluorescence color photographs of the probe in the presence of several interferences (100 μM).

(a) Possible detection mechanism between DAP with DCP, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD+DCP and DCP, (c) Fluorescence decay curve of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at 570 nm before and after exposure to DCP.

(a) Detection device for DCP vapor, (b) Schematic diagram of quantitative fluorescence colorimetric card for DCP vapor detection by Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogel, (c) G+B-R values of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogels under different concentrations of DCP vapor. Inset: G+B-R relationship of the composite hydrogel to DCP vapor, (d) G+B-R response of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogels to DCP and interfering vapors.
Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD based sensor and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD loaded agarose hydrogel portable kit for DCP vapor detection.

head.ce:title: Cu2+-functionalized Zr-MOF triggers O-phenylendiamine oxidation for ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of nerve agent simulant

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a fluorescence sensor capable of precisely identifying highly toxic nerve agents has become a focus of research, given that they are easily available and can be used by terrorists to threaten the security of the world. In this work, we designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform with Cu2+-modified UiO-66-NH2 (Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2) and o-phenenediamine (OPD) as signal indicators and successfully used for the liquid and gas detection of the nerve agent simulant diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP). UiO-66-NH2 obtained oxidase activity by simple Cu2+ modification, which catalyzed the oxidation of OPD to give a yellow fluorescent product 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP). In this detection system, the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2 was greatly enhanced by blocking the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) process inside the framework by DCP. At the same time, the amino group of DAP was phosphorylated by DCP, and the attenuation of DAP fluorescence was observed by the static quenching effect. In addition, the Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD loaded agarose hydrogel device can be used to detect DCP vapor with naked eyes and showed satisfactory detection result (LOD: 6.65 ppb).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nerve agents are a group of highly toxic, highly reactive and lethal chemical warfare agents that have been used in terrorism and regional wars [1]. Nerve agents containing phosphate or phosphite groups bind to the serine hydroxyl group of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) to restrain the activity of AChE thereby disrupting nerve conduction and eventually leading to death [2,3]. Although the use of nerve agents has been clearly prohibited in the world, they have continued to occur in wars and conflicts over the past decades, posing a grievous threat to national and public security [4,5]. To reduce possible casualties, developing a simple and efficient method to detect nerve agents is of extremely essential [6]. So far, a variety of nerve agents detection techniques have been reported, including electrochemical sensors [7–9], Surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [10], mass spectrometry/gas chromatography [11,12], etc. However, these detection methods have the disadvantages of cumbersome processing, high cost, and poor portability.

In contrast, fluorescent probes have been extensively studied for their advantages, especially their unique advantages on handheld devices [13–15]. Due to the simplicity, sensitivity and specificity of fluorescence technology, a series of fluorescent sensors were developed and reported to realize the sensitive detection of nerve agents in liquid phase and gas phase [16–20]. However, they mainly rely on organic fluorescent small molecules, which still have some disadvantages, such as complex synthesis steps, easy aggregation in water and cytotoxicity, etc [21]. To avoid these problems, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted the extensive attention of researchers in the field of sensing because of their superiorities of simple synthesis, high reactivity, environmental friendliness, and adjustable porosity [22]. MOFs are a kind of porous crystalline materials that are autonomously assembled of metal ions and organic ligands, which confer excellent optical properties to MOFs [23]. So far, MOFs have been used in temperature sensing, ion sensing, biomarker sensing, and pH sensing [24–27]. It has been reported that Zirconium-based MOF materials have high thermal and chemical stability, in which UiO-66-NH2 showing an excellent response to phosphate due to its high sensitivity and ability to coordinate specifically between Zirconium and phosphate, holds promise as a fluorescence sensing material for the recognition of nerve agents containing phosphate groups [28,29]. Furthermore, development of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform with reverse variation of dual emission can reduce systematic errors and environmental effects compared to a single emission as output [30–32]. Therefore, it is of great significance to quantitatively detect DCP using ratiometric fluorescent probes based on dual-emission band detection.

Inspired by the above, this paper constructed a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 catalyzed oxidation of O-phenendiamine (OPD) to achieve the sensitive detection of the nerve agent simulant diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP). As illustrated in Scheme 1, the Zr-O node of UiO-66-NH2 could trap the phosphate group in DCP, the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) was impaired, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence of ligand 2-amino-terphenyl acid (BDC-NH2) at 450 nm [33,34]. On the other hand, OPD as a fluorescent chromogenic substrate was oxidized by Cu2+ to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), and the new emission peak appeared at 570 nm [35,36]. Since the target DCP reacted with DAP to form a non-fluorescent complex, the fluorescence of DAP at 570 nm was quenched by DCP. Using this strategy, we designed a novel fluorescent sensor Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, which exhibited excellent luminescence characteristics and achieved sensitive detection of DCP in liquid phase. Subsequently, fluorescent hydrogels were obtained by loading fluorescent probe into agarose hydrogels. The hydrogel was integrated with a smart phone portable sensing platform to capture fluorescent color pictures, and Image J software was used to digitally transform the corresponding images to achieve sensitive monitoring of DCP vapor. The availability of this portable sensor provided a new approach to the on-site detection of DCP vapor.

All experiment reagents and chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and were used directly without further treatment and purification. Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O), triethyl phosphate (TEP) and N-hexane (C6H12) were obtained from Sinopdrug Group Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Agarose, Zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4), triethyl phosphite (TMPi), diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP), diethyl cyanine phosphate (DCNP), dimethyl methylphosphonate (DMMP), trimethyl phosphate (TMP) were gained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). O-phenylene diamine (OPD) was gained from China Wulian Chemical Plant (Shanghai, China), 2-aminoterylene acid (BDC-NH2) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China), and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) was purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were gained on a F-4600 fluorometer and a Shimadzu UV-1900i spectrophotometer, severally. Fluorescence lifetimes were collected on a FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was measured on a SMartLab9KW powder X-ray diffractometer. The morphology of the material was characterized using a ZEISS Gemini 300 scanning electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed on a Fourier IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. Fluorescence color photographs of the composite hydrogels were taken by a smartphone (Remi K40S, China).

UiO-66-NH2 was prepared following the previously reported method [37], 126 mg ZrCl4 was mixed with 10 mL DMF and 1 mL concentrated HCl. Then 136 mg BDC-NH2 and 5 mL DMF were added to the above mixture, and after mixing evenly, the reaction was carried out for 18 h at 80 °C. After reaction, pale yellow solid was collected by centrifugation and washing several times with DMF and ethanol, severally. Lastly, the UiO-66-NH2 obtained was placed in a vacuum drying oven for 18 h at 80 °C.

Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 was prepared by double-solvents method [38]. In brief, 300 mg UiO-66-NH2 was dissolved in 80 mL N-hexane and sonicated until completely dissolved. Then 500 μL of aqueous Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O (1.1 mg) solution was mixed well with the above mixture under vigorous stirring and stirred for 6 h at 25 ℃ to obtain a light green solid. The resulting light green precipitate was then collected by centrifuging for 10 min at 12,000 rpm and washing several times with deionized water, and then dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 ℃ overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence analysis of DCP in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: 30 μL Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg/mL), 5 μL OPD (5 mM) and 955 μL deionized water were heated and reacted for 1 h at 90 °C to form pale yellow solution. Then the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature, 10 μL DCP (10 mM) acetonitrile solution was injected to the above solutions, the fluorescence emission spectra were performed at 255 nm excitation.

50 uL OPD (5 mM) and 200 uL Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg/mL) were injected to 1.75 mL of deionized water and heated at 90 ℃ for 1 h to form a uniform yellow solution. 0.05 g agarose was weighed and added to the above solution, heated until completely dissolved and transferred to a petri dish to cool to gel. A 5 mL centrifuge tube cap was used to press into round hydrogel sheets of the same size for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DCP vapor detection using Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The saturated vapor pressure of DCP at room temperature is approximately 132 ppm, which can be achieved by adding 1 mL of pure DCP into a 50 mL centrifuge tube [39]. The hydrogel sheet was set in a centrifuge tube of the same size in advance, and then different volumes of saturated steam were injected to obtain different concentrations of DCP steam. After 1.5 min of reaction, the hydrogel sheets were removed and photographed by a smartphone under 254 nm UV lamp at night in a dark paper box, and the position and angle of the smartphone from the hydrogel sheets were also fixed, and the RGB values were analyzed by Image J software.

Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized by the two-solvent method and the morphology of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 was first explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fig. 1a depicted that it had a spherical nanostructure and smooth surface similar to UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S1a). To obtain size information of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, we performed statistical analysis of particle size on SEM images. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, especially when Cu2+ mass was 1% of UiO-66-NH2 mass, the crystal size of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 was about 140 nm, which was comparable in size to the initial UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S1b). So as to verify the successful synthesis of the composite, SEM was used to perform elemental composition analysis, and the coexistence of C, N, O, Cu and Zr proved the uniform distribution of elements in the whole Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2. (Fig. 1c). At the same time, the PXRD experiment well showed that the Cu2+ doping did not destroy the crystal structure of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 1d). The optical properties of the sample were studied by emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectra in water. From the emission spectrum (Fig. S1c), it was found that the Cu2+ modification would weaken the emission intensity of UiO-66-NH2, which might be due to Cu2+ forming Cu-amine complexes with amino groups on the surface of UiO-66-NH2 [40]. Fig. S1d showed that Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2 showed semblable results, with absorption in the visible range. The peak intensity of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 is higher than that of UiO-66-NH2 at the same concentration, indicating that the visible light absorption ability of the sample can be improved by the addition of Cu2+.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further demonstrated the successful doping of Cu2+ in UiO-66-NH2 and determined the chemical states of each element. Fig. 2a showed the XPS measurement scans of UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 which represented Cu 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s and Zr 3d respectively. N 1 s XPS spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 were divided into several symmetric peaks, and the results were appeared in Fig. 2b, with peaks centered at 399.7 eV and 400.4 eV associated with C-N and N-H, respectively [41]. In contrast to UiO-66-NH2, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 showed a characteristic peak of Cu-N at 398.9 eV, which indicated that Cu2+ and UiO-66-NH2 were bound together by Cu-N coordination [42]. In the meantime, the characteristic peak of Cu-N at 1084 cm-1 in Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra also well proved the formation of Cu-N bond [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence and absorption properties of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated the luminescence and absorption properties of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD. Fig. 3a showed that the fluorescence spectra of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 had a weak emission at 455 nm. Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 catalyzed oxidation of OPD appeared a new emission peak at 570 nm, which was consistent with the emission peak position of DAP. The fluorescence peak position of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at 450 nm was slightly blue shifted compared with Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, which could be the interaction between OPD and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 in the composite [44]. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3b) was investigated, and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 possessed a wide absorption band centered at 380 nm, which was assigned to the interaction between the single pair of electrons on the amino group of BDC-NH2 and the π * orbital of the benzene ring [45]. For DAP, clear absorption peaks were observed at 258 nm and 435 nm, corresponding to the π-π * and n-π * electron transitions on the benzene ring, respectively [46,47]. For Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, the characteristic absorption peaks of both DAP and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 were identified. At the same time, the emission peak and UV–vis absorption peak of OPD after reacting with Cu2+ were similar to those of DAP, indicating that Cu2+ could catalyze the oxidation of OPD (Fig. S2). Overall, the above conclusions clearly showed that Cu2+ in Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 catalyzed the successful oxidation of OPD to DAP, and it could be identified from Fig. 3a that Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD had a clear response to DCP, constructing a promising ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral response of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD to DCP in solution
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal sensing ability of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, the reaction conditions were systematically investigated. Fig. S3a showed the fluorescence response gradually weakened as the loading of Cu2+ increased, and 1% was chosen as the optimal loading of Cu2+ considering the catalytic performance. Fig. S3b showed that the optimal sensitivity of the response to DCP was achieved when the chroma of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 was 30 μg/mL. In addition, the DCP reaction time plot (Fig. S3c) showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F450 nm/F570 nm) was mostly stable at 1 min

The response of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 to the DCP concentration range was recorded under optimum sensing conditions. As shown in Fig. 4a, with the increase of DCP concentration (0–100 μM) in the system, the fluorescence intensity of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at 450 nm progressively increased with a slight red shift, while the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm progressively decreased, which resulted in a gradual increase in the ratio of F450 nm/F570 nm. The fluorescence color of this solution gradually transformed from yellow to blue as seen with the naked eye (Fig. 4a, inset), indicating the potential of this sensor to visually quantify DCP concentrations. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with DCP concentration (2.5–70 μM) (R2 =0.995), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.25 μM calculated from the titration curve (Fig. 4b). To evaluate the target specificity of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, various analytes were tested in aqueous solution, such as trimethyl phosphate (TMP), triethyl phosphite (TMPi), triethyl phosphate (TEP), diethyl methylphosphonate (DMEP), dimethyl methylphosphonate (DMMP) and diethyl cyanophosphonate (DCNP). The emission spectra in Fig. 4c and the relative fluorescence intensity plots in Fig. 4d showed that the presence of DCP prompted a large variation in fluorescence intensity in this system. After adding other interfering substances, the fluorescence intensity almost showed a negative response. These experimental results demonstrated the remarkable selectivity of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD for DCP and its potential application in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD for DCP Detection
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain information about the sensing mechanism, we performed a set of experiments to explore the relation between DCP and Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD. The mechanism of the fluorescence change at 450 nm was first investigated. As reflected in Fig. S4a, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD reacted with DCP without obvious changes in the crystal structure, ruling out the effect caused by structural collapse. The FT-IR spectrogram of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD showed the appearance of a tensile vibrational peak of the P-O bond at 1044 cm-1 after exposure to DCP (Fig. S4b), which proved the strong coordination of the DCP with the Zr-O site in Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD [48]. In addition, comparison was made between the alterations in fluorescence lifetime of the system before and after adding DCP. As can be seen in Fig. S4c, the fluorescence lifetime at 450 nm increased from 11.34 ns to 18.48 ns with the additon of DCP. These results indicated that DCP was coordinated with Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2, blocking the LMCT process and leading to the fluorescence recovery of the material at 450 nm. Next, we explored the fluorescence quenching mechanism at 570 nm, Fig. S5a showed that the UV–vis absorption spectrum was red-shifted after DAP reacted with DCP, and the emission spectra in Fig. S5b showed that DCP could promote the fluorescence quenching of DAP. Then the reaction mixture of DAP and DCP was next studied by 1H NMR in DMSO-d6. Fig. S6a showed the 1H NMR spectrum of DAP without DCP, and the chemical shift at 6.27 ppm was caused by -NH2-. The addition of DCP resulted in a nucleophilic substitution of DCP with DAP, the chemical shift at 6.27 ppm disappeared, and a new peak representing -NH- appeared at 6.59 ppm (Fig. S6b). 1H NMR spectra showed that a nucleophilic reaction occurred between DCP and DAP to form the corresponding phosphorylation product ( Fig. 5a). In the UV–vis absorption spectra, the peaks of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at 259 nm and 420 nm were redshifted with the addition of DCP (Fig. 5b), which also well illustrated the generation of the non-fluorescent complex DAP-DCP. Next, the fluorescence lifetime of the probe before and after the addition of DCP was tested, and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching at 570 nm was further explored. As seen in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence lifetime of this probe was not altered by the injection of DCP, implying that the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD at 570 nm by DCP may be static quenching [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DCP vapor by Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the outstanding optical properties of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD, Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogel was synthesized using agarose hydrogel as the carrier, and the experimental conditions were optimized (Fig. S7) to assess the practical application of the sensing method in gaseous nerve agents. Sensing experiments were performed using the setup shown in Fig. 6a, as shown in Fig. 6b, when composite hydrogel exposed to DCP vapor in the concentration range of 0–132 ppm, the fluorescent color picture showed that fluorescent color of the composite hydrogel gradually changed from tawny to dark blue. The lowest concentration of DCP vapor could be determined to be 0.01 ppm by naked eye resolution of the fluorescent color picture, and the visual detection of low concentration of nerve agent vapor could be realized by this method. Then the fluorescence pictures of the composite hydrogel were analyzed by RGB values. Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD composite hydrogel showed high sensitivity to DCP vapor and a gradient response. The G+B-R value showed an excellent linear relationship with the DCP vapor concentration in the range of 0.001–0.1 ppm (Fig. 6c). The detection limit of DCP vapor can be as low as 6.65 ppb, calculated from the relation equation, which is very significant compared to previously reported sensors (Table S1). In addition, 6.65 ppb is lower than the IDLH (immediate life-threatening or health threatening) concentration of sarin (7 ppb) [51], highlighting the advantages of Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD in field detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the composite hydrogel to possible interferents was studied. As shown in Fig. 6d, DCP vapor can cause the change of the fluorescence color of the composite hydrogel from tawny to blue, while other organic phosphorus and acid gases such as H2O, HCl, TMPi, TEP, TMP, DMMP and DCNP can cause negligible changes in the hydrogel. These observations suggested that agarose hydrogels loaded with Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2/OPD showed great potential for real-time discrimination of nerve agent in the gas phase.

On the whole, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Cu2+@UiO-66-NH2 and successfully detected the nerve agent mimetic DCP. Firstly, UiO-66-NH2 had weak fluorescence intensity due to the LMCT process, and the binding of Zr-O site to DCP blocked the LMCT process, which promoted the emission intensity recovery of BDC-NH2 at the 450 nm emission peak. Meanwhile, Cu2+ catalyzed the oxidation of OPD to DAP with yellow fluorescence, and DCP induced phosphorylation enhanced the static quenching effect, resulting in a fluorescence off behavior at the emission peak of 570 nm. Finally, agarose hydrogels loaded with fluorescent probe were prepared. When the composite hydrogel was exposed to trace DCP vapor, the fluorescent color of the composite hydrogel altered from tawny to blue was easily discerned with naked eye. Fluorescence sensing platform based on metal-organic frameworks not only showed outstanding potential in nerve agent detection, but also provided effective strategies and insights for realizing rapid and sensitive and visual detection of nerve agents.

You Lei: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yuting Gao: Data-measurement, Methodology. Yi Xiao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Pengcheng Huang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Fang-Ying Wu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No.22164013, 21864018), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No.20212BAB203033).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134553.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Destruction and detection of chemical warfare agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kim, O.G. Tsay, D.A. Atwood, D.G. Churchill, Destruction and Detection of Chemical Warfare Agents, Chem. Rev., 111 (2011), pp. 5345–5403. 10.1021/cr100193y.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of acetylcholinesterase protection against sarin and soman by adenosine A1 receptor agonist N6-cyclopentyladenosine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Beste, D.E. Taylor, T.M. Shih, T.P. Thomas, Mechanisms of acetylcholinesterase protection against sarin and soman by adenosine A1 receptor agonist N6-cyclopentyladenosine, Comput. Biol. Chem., 75 (2018), pp. 74–81. 10.1016/j.compbiolchem.2018.04.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two birds with one stone: The detection of nerve agents and AChE activity with an ICT-ESIPT-based fluorescence sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.Q. Meng, Z.P. Pei, Y.R. Wang, M.X. Sun, Q.Q. Xu, J.F. Cen, K. Guo, K. Xiao, Z.J. Li, Two birds with one stone: The detection of nerve agents and AChE activity with an ICT-ESIPT-based fluorescence sensor, J. Hazard. Mater., 410 (2021), p. 124811. 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.124811.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengcheng Huang is a professor of School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, China. He earned his B.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees from Northwest University (2009), and Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (2014) under the supervision of Prof. Lanqun Mao, respectively. He was a visiting scholar in Prof. Jing Li’s group in the Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology at Rutgers University, USA, in 2018–2019. His current research interests are focused on carbon-based nanomaterials, noble-metal nanoparticles/nanoclusters and metal-organic frameworks-based fluorescent and colorimetric probes for biosensing and bioimaging, environmental monitoring, and food quality assessment.

Fang-Ying Wu is currently a professor of School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, China. She received her B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees from Jiujiang Normal College (1987) and Nanchang University (1996), respectively. She obtained her Ph.D. in Xiamen University in 2003 and pursued her postdoctoral studies at Department of Chemistry, Seoul National University (2005–2006). Her work mainly focuses on design, synthesis, and applications of novel organic dyes- and nanomaterials-based fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for metal ions, anions, and biomolecules.
